sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=32, col=4), MOUSE(row=32, col=4).
You have progressed to a new level!
Current state: step 8, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-5): Clicking red block (row~32,col~4) rotates all 20 perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. 20 colored 4x4 blocks (16px each) rotate around perimeter. 4 yellow 2x2 markers (4px each) fixed near top-left corner. Black 2x2 blocks in HUD top row.
- HUD map: Top gray area (rows 0-12) with 8 black 2x2 blocks at row 1, cols 10/15/20/25/30/35/40/45. Left green strip (col 0). Charcoal background (1962px). HUD black blocks appear unchanged across rotations.
- Win pattern: TBD - must be a specific rotation state. All 20 rotations have identical grouping (2 adjacent-same pairs, 18 transitions). No rotation has special symmetry.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

[LEDGER COMPACTION]
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
Current level:
- World model: Level 2 has different layout: 3 rows of colored blocks in a 41x41 charcoal area. Red blocks at (25,13) and (34,13) likely triggers. Green blocks at (25,47) and (34,47) likely decorations. Multiple colored 2x2 blocks arranged in rows. Yellow markers at specific positions. Need to determine rotation mechanic for this layout.
- Goal model: Likely same as Level 1: rotate until progress bar depletes. Need to verify rotation behavior on new layout.
- Recent findings: ['Level 1 completed by 8 rotations', 'Level 2 has 3-row layout with multiple red and green blocks', 'Green strip still at col 0 (64px) - resets per level']
- Open questions: ['Which red block is the trigger in Level 2?', 'What blocks rotate in Level 2?', 'Same rotation direction?']
- Plan: Test clicking red blocks to find trigger, then rotate until completion like Level 1
- Strategy log: ['Level 1: grouping/symmetry analysis abandoned - all rotations equal. Solved by rotating until strip depleted.']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(32,54) green block: no effect (step 4)', 'MOUSE(20,13) colored block: no effect (step 5)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at Level 2, I see a different layout. From the segmentation data I got (though truncated), I can see:
- Red blocks at (16,19), (25,13), and (34,13) - these are 4x3, 10px each
- Green blocks at (16,38), (25,47), and (34,47) - these are 4x3, 10px each
- Many 2x2 colored blocks arranged in rows
- Yellow 1x1 markers at various positions
- A green strip at col 0 (64px)
The layout seems to have 3 horizontal rows of blocks. Let me focus on understanding the structure and testing the red block trigger.
From the truncated output, I can see the red blocks are at:
- id=11: R, (16,19), 4x3
- id=22: R, (25,13), 4x3
- id=46: R, (34,13), 4x3
And green blocks:
- id=12: N, (16,38), 4x3
- id=26: N, (25,47), 4x3
- id=50: N, (34,47), 4x3
In Level 1, the red block was at (32,4) which was on the left edge. Here we have red blocks at different positions. Let me try clicking the leftmost red block first (at col 13) to see if it triggers rotation.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, I see:
- Top row: red block, then colored blocks, then green block
- Middle row: red block, then colored blocks, then green block
- Bottom row: red block, then colored blocks, then green block
The red blocks seem to be on the left side of each row, and green blocks on the right. In Level 1, clicking the red block rotated all perimeter blocks. Here, maybe clicking a red block rotates the blocks in that row?
Let me test by clicking one of the red blocks and seeing what changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Level 2: Test clicking red block to see what rotates
# Red blocks at: (16,19), (25,13), (34,13)
# Try the middle red block first (25, 13)

# Save current state for comparison
seg_before = current_frame.segmentation
nodes_before = {n['id']: (n['color'], n['hash'], [min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])]) for n in seg_before['nodes']}

# Click the middle red block
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 13}])

# Compare
seg_after = current_frame.segmentation
nodes_after = {n['id']: (n['color'], n['hash'], [min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])]) for n in seg_after['nodes']}

# Find changes
changed = []
for nid in nodes_after:
    if nid not in nodes_before:
        changed.append(f"New: id={nid} {nodes_after[nid]}")
    elif nodes_after[nid] != nodes_before[nid]:
        changed.append(f"Changed: id={nid} {nodes_before[nid]} -> {nodes_after[nid]}")

print(f"Changed nodes: {len(changed)}")
for c in changed[:20]:
    print(f"  {c}")

# Check green strip
green_nodes = [n for n in seg_after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"Green strip: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: Red block rotates perimeter clockwise. Green strip shrinks 5px/rotation.", "object_taxonomy": "Red=trigger, Green strip=progress bar, colored blocks=rotating objects", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks with rotations", "win_pattern": "Rotate until green strip depletes", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rotations to deplete 64px strip", "cross_level_notes": "Same mechanic across levels"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "3 rows of blocks with red triggers on left, green on right", "goal_model": "Rotate until strip depletes", "recent_findings": ["Level 2 has 3 red blocks and 3 green blocks"], "open_questions": ["Which red block triggers rotation?", "What rotates?"], "current_plan": "Test middle red block (25,13)", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<parameter=code>
# Level 2: Test clicking red block to see what rotates
# Red blocks at: (16,19), (25,13), (34,13)

# Save current state for comparison
